Summary: This retrospective study investigates the diagnostic and prognostic value of conventional MRI radiomics in differentiating between early progression (EP) and pseudoprogression (Psp) in glioblastoma patients after radiochemotherapy. The study found that radiomics, especially when combined with MGMT promoter status, shows promising diagnostic value, albeit with moderate specificity. Additionally, the study suggests the potential of radiomics in predicting overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS).

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual segmentation of the contrast-enhanced tumoral portion and peritumoral parenchymal area on MRI images to extract radiomic features.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by an expert reader, indicating that it was not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts 39 radiomics features per volume of interest, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses radiomic features that are normalized and likely organized in a table for analysis.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it relies on hand-crafted feature extraction and a random forest classifier.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines clear inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard (EP vs. Psp) is based on histological analysis or radioclinical follow-up according to RANO criteria, which is a reasonable approach in the absence of a definitive diagnostic method.

Item4.reason: The study is conducted at a single institution, so it is not multi-center.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is conventional MRI, which is a standard imaging technique for glioblastoma evaluation.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters for both 1.5 Tesla and 3 Tesla MRI scans, including sequence details, TE, TR, TI, FA, and section thickness.

Item7.reason: The study includes patients who experienced radiological progression within 6 months of their concomitant TMZ radiochemotherapy, which is an appropriate interval for evaluating EP vs. Psp.

Item8.reason: The study describes the manual segmentation methodology, specifying the volumes of interest (T1CE, FT, FO, FOT) and the use of T1CE and FLAIR images.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by a single expert reader.

Item11.reason: The study mentions normalization of radiomics features to z-scores, which is an appropriate preprocessing technique. They also specify the MRI sequences used (FLAIR, T1CE).

Item12.reason: The study used LifeX software for feature extraction and the software is cited.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (radiomics features) and mentions that 39 radiomics features were extracted per volume. Supplementary tables provide additional details.

Item14.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the removal of non-robust features. Therefore, this item is not applicable.

Item15.reason: The study uses a Wilcoxon-test-based method and supervised principal component analysis (SPCA) to select the most informative features, indicating dimensionality reduction.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size. There is no statistical evidence or reference to previous literature to support this.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions random assignment of patients to training and validation groups in a 2:1 ratio, maintaining the EP/Psp ratio. They also perform data splitting before processing.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address or analyze potential confounding factors such as scanner type or other patient characteristics.

Item20.reason: The study reports accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for the classification model, and C-index and IBS for the survival models, which are appropriate performance evaluation metrics. Kaplan-Meier plots are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals for accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, as well as for hazard ratios.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention any calibration assessment of the final models.

Item23.reason: The study uses both FLAIR and contrast-enhanced T1-weighted images. There is no comparison to uni-parametric imaging to justify the use of multi-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study does not compare the radiomics approach with a non-radiomic method representative of clinical practice. There is no proof of added clinical value demonstrated.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the results with a simple or classical statistical model.

Item26.reason: The study tests the model on an independent validation set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing on data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data availability.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code availability.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention any model availability.
